section.title: The Shepherds of Israel
section.sectionRationale: This chapter is the summit of Ezekiel's turn from judgment to restoration: after Israel's failed shepherds are indicted for feeding themselves instead of the flock, the LORD Himself promises to search for His scattered sheep, and then sets over them 'My servant David', one shepherd who fulfils what every human shepherd could not.
section.thesis: Because Israel's own shepherds fed themselves and scattered the flock, the LORD Himself becomes their Shepherd, seeking the lost and binding up the injured, and appoints His servant David as the one shepherd who will feed them under a covenant of peace, a promise fulfilled in Jesus Christ, the Good Shepherd.
section.frameNote: Four movements: the woeful indictment of Israel's self-serving shepherds; the LORD's own promise to search for, gather, and tend His flock; His just judgment between the fat sheep and the lean; and the appointment of one shepherd, His servant David, under an everlasting covenant of peace.

units.label: Woe to Israel's shepherds
units.note: leaders who fed themselves instead of the flock

verses.text: Then the word of the LORD came to me, saying,

“Son of man, prophesy against the {{roeh|shepherds}} of Israel. Prophesy and tell them that this is what the Lord GOD says: ‘Woe to the shepherds of Israel, who only feed themselves! Should not the shepherds feed their flock?
You eat the fat, wear the wool, and butcher the fattened sheep, but you do not feed the flock.
You have not strengthened the weak, healed the sick, bound up the injured, brought back the strays, or searched for the lost. Instead, you have ruled them with violence and cruelty.
They were scattered for lack of a shepherd, and when they were scattered they became food for all the wild beasts.
My flock went astray on all the mountains and every high hill. They were scattered over the face of all the earth, with no one to search for them or seek them out.’

units.label: I Myself will search for My flock

verses.text: Therefore, you shepherds, hear the word of the LORD:

‘As surely as I live, declares the Lord GOD, because My flock lacks a shepherd and has become prey and food for every wild beast, and because My shepherds did not search for My flock but fed themselves instead,
therefore, you shepherds, hear the word of the LORD!’

This is what the Lord GOD says: ‘Behold, I am against the shepherds, and I will demand from them My flock and remove them from tending the flock, so that they can no longer feed themselves. For I will deliver My flock from their mouths, and it will no longer be food for them.’
For this is what the Lord GOD says: ‘Behold, I Myself will search for My flock and seek them out.

As a shepherd looks for his scattered sheep when he is among the flock, so I will look for My flock. 
I will rescue them from all the places to which they were scattered on a day of clouds and darkness.

I will bring them out from the peoples, gather them from the countries, and bring them into their own land. I will feed them on the mountains of Israel, in the ravines, and in all the settlements of the land.

I will feed them in good pasture, and the lofty mountains of Israel will be their grazing land. There they will lie down in a good grazing land; they will feed in rich pasture on the mountains of Israel.
I will tend My flock and make them lie down, declares the Lord GOD.
I will seek the lost, bring back the strays, bind up the broken, and strengthen the weak; but the sleek and strong I will destroy. I will shepherd them with justice.’

units.label: Judgment between sheep and sheep
units.note: the fat, pushy sheep and the lean, trampled ones

verses.text: This is what the Lord GOD says to you, My flock: ‘I will judge between one sheep and another, between the rams and the goats.

Is it not enough for you to feed on the good pasture? Must you also trample the rest of the pasture with your feet? Is it not enough for you to drink the clear waters? Must you also muddy the rest with your feet?
Why must My flock feed on what your feet have trampled, and drink what your feet have muddied?’
Therefore this is what the Lord GOD says to them: ‘Behold, I Myself will judge between the fat sheep and the lean sheep.
Since you shove with flank and shoulder, butting all the weak ones with your horns until you have scattered them abroad,
I will save My flock, and they will no longer be prey. I will judge between one sheep and another.

units.label: One shepherd, My servant David
units.note: the appointed shepherd and the covenant of peace

verses.text: I will appoint over them one shepherd, My {{eved_david|servant David}}, and he will feed them. He will feed them and be their shepherd.
I, the LORD, will be their God, and My servant David will be a prince among them. I, the LORD, have spoken.
I will make with them a {{berit_shalom|covenant of peace}} and rid the land of wild animals, so that they may dwell securely in the wilderness and sleep in the forest.
I will make them and the places around My hill a blessing. I will send down showers in season, showers of blessing.

The trees of the field will give their fruit, and the land will yield its produce; My flock will be secure in their land. Then they will know that I am the LORD, when I have broken the bars of their yoke and delivered them from the hands that enslaved them.
They will no longer be prey for the nations, and the beasts of the earth will not consume them. They will dwell securely, and no one will frighten them.
And I will raise up for them a garden of renown, and they will no longer be victims of famine in the land or bear the scorn of the nations.
Then they will know that I, the LORD their God, am with them, and that they, the house of Israel, are My people,’ declares the Lord GOD.
‘You are My flock, the sheep of My pasture, My people, and I am your God,’ declares the Lord GOD.”

roeh.gloss: Shepherd, a common ancient Near Eastern title for a king's duty to protect and provide for his people. Israel's kings, priests, and civic leaders are indicted under this title for feeding themselves instead of the flock.

eved_david.gloss: 'My servant David.' Since David had already died centuries before Ezekiel, this is a prophetic title for the coming heir of David's throne, the Messiah, who finally does what David and every other shepherd-king could not.

berit_shalom.gloss: Covenant of peace, a single, comprehensive covenant binding together security, provision, and right relationship with God into one lasting promise, echoed again in Ezekiel 37:26.

basic.context: Israel's leaders, the 'shepherds', have fed themselves at the flock's expense: eating the fat, wearing the wool, ruling with force, never healing the sick or seeking the lost. So the sheep scattered and became prey. In response, God does something this whole book has been building toward: He says, 'I Myself will search for My flock,' and then appoints 'My servant David' as the one shepherd who will tend them under an everlasting covenant of peace.
basic.hermeneutics: This is real judgment on Israel's actual historical kings, priests, and civic leaders, using shepherd imagery that was a standard ancient Near Eastern description of a king's duty. Its christological weight is not read into the text; the New Testament itself identifies Jesus as the fulfillment of the promised shepherd, most explicitly in John 10.
basic.originalLanguages: Israel's failed leaders are ro'im, shepherds, a common royal title for a king's duty to protect and provide. Against their failure, God promises one shepherd, His eved David, servant David, and a berit shalom, covenant of peace, binding promises of security and blessing into a single lasting bond.
basic.typology: David had been dead for centuries by Ezekiel's day, so 'My servant David' points beyond the historical king to a coming heir of David's line who will finally do what David and every other shepherd-king could not. Jesus says directly, 'I am the good shepherd. The good shepherd lays down his life for the sheep,' John 10:11, and 'there shall be one flock and one shepherd,' John 10:16, echoing Ezekiel 34:23-24 precisely.
basic.christ: Jesus explicitly claims the identity this chapter promises. Where Israel's shepherds fed themselves and scattered the flock, Jesus feeds His sheep and lays down His life for them, John 10:11-15. Where God said, 'I Myself will search for My flock and seek them out' (v11), Jesus tells the parable of the lost sheep and says the Son of Man came to seek and to save the lost, Luke 15:4-7, 19:10. He is both the LORD who Himself shepherds His flock and the appointed servant-shepherd descended from David, uniting deity and Davidic humanity in one Person.
basic.god: God is a jealous Shepherd, personally offended when His sheep are fed on by those He entrusted with their care; He judges the false shepherds directly, 'Behold, I am against the shepherds' (v10), and He judges even within the flock, between the fat, pushy sheep and the lean, trampled ones (vv17-22). Yet His anger at bad shepherding flows from tender, personal love: He searches, gathers, binds up, strengthens, and finally shepherds them Himself.
basic.crossReferences: John 10:11-16 shows Jesus claiming the title and role this chapter promises directly. Luke 15:4-7 dramatizes God's promise to seek the lost sheep Himself. 1 Peter 2:25 applies this chapter's language directly to Christ as Shepherd and Overseer of souls. Revelation 7:17 shows the Lamb Himself becoming the flock's Shepherd forever.
basic.oneStory: This is the summit of the turn toward restoration: failed human shepherds are judged, and God Himself steps in to seek, gather, and tend His scattered flock, appointing His servant David as the one shepherd under an everlasting covenant of peace. Every page after this runs toward the Good Shepherd, Jesus Christ, who lays down His life for the sheep and gathers one flock under Himself forever.

context.literary: One of Ezekiel's most sustained extended metaphors, moving from indictment (shepherds, verses 1-6) to divine promise (verses 7-22) to climactic appointment of a coming shepherd-king under an everlasting covenant (verses 23-31).
context.historical: Addressed to a specific failure of Judah's kings, priests, and civic leaders in the years leading up to the exile, whose corruption and violence Ezekiel has already detailed at length (chapters 19, 21-23), now named plainly as shepherds who fed themselves.
context.canonical: One of the Old Testament's clearest messianic shepherd texts, standing alongside Micah 5:2-4, Jeremiah 23:1-6, and Zechariah 13:7, and directly claimed as fulfilled by Jesus in John 10.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To indict Israel's failed leaders for exploiting the very people they were meant to protect, and to promise that God Himself will shepherd His flock and appoint a coming Davidic shepherd-king to feed them under an everlasting covenant of peace.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The chapter describes specific historical failures of leadership and a specific covenant promise; it is not prescribing a system of government. Its enduring, prescriptive truth is the pattern of true leadership as sacrificial care rather than self-serving power, and the certainty of God's promised shepherd-king.

debates.question: Who is 'My servant David' in verses 23-24: the historical King David risen again, a future Davidic descendant, or a title reserved for the Messiah alone?

views.label: A prophetic title for the Messiah, David's greater Son
views.case: Most read 'My servant David' as prophetic shorthand for the coming Messiah as the ultimate heir of David's throne (compare Jeremiah 30:9, Hosea 3:5), since David had already died and this shepherd's reign is described as everlasting, a scope only Christ fulfills.

views.label: A representative Davidic ruler distinct from Christ alone
views.case: A few older readings connect this more narrowly to a restored Davidic governor at the return from exile, seeing the fullest, final meaning unfold only later in the New Testament's application to Jesus, without every detail necessarily intended messianically from the start.

debates.bearing: Both readings converge in Christ: whether read as directly messianic from the start or fulfilled progressively, the New Testament identifies Jesus, David's greater Son, as the one shepherd finally set over God's one flock.

study.originalLanguages: Israel's failed leaders are ro'im, shepherds, a common royal title across the ancient Near East for a king's duty to protect and provide for his people. Against their failure, God promises to give them one shepherd, His eved David, servant David, and to make with them a berit shalom, covenant of peace, binding promises of security and blessing into a single lasting bond.

typology.type: The LORD Himself searching for and shepherding His scattered flock, Ezekiel 34:11-16
typology.fulfillment: Fulfilled in Jesus, who tells the parable of the shepherd seeking the one lost sheep, Luke 15:4-7, and declares that the Son of Man came to seek and to save the lost, Luke 19:10.

typology.type: One shepherd, My servant David, ruling under a covenant of peace, Ezekiel 34:23-25
typology.fulfillment: Fulfilled in Jesus Christ, the Son of David and the good shepherd who lays down His life for the sheep and unites them into one flock, John 10:11-16.

study.christ: Jesus explicitly claims the identity this chapter promises. Where Israel's shepherds fed themselves and scattered the flock, Jesus feeds His sheep and lays down His life for them, John 10:11-15. Where God said, 'I Myself will search for My flock and seek them out' (v11), Jesus tells the parable of the lost sheep and declares that the Son of Man came to seek and to save the lost, Luke 15:4-7, 19:10. Where God promised one shepherd, His servant David, ruling as prince under a covenant of peace (vv23-25), Jesus is the Son of David who reigns forever and makes peace by the blood of His cross, Colossians 1:20, Luke 1:32-33. He is both the LORD who Himself shepherds His flock and the appointed servant-shepherd descended from David, uniting deity and Davidic humanity in one Person, exactly as this chapter holds both together without explaining how, until the incarnation resolves it.
study.god: God is a jealous Shepherd, personally offended when His sheep are fed on by those He entrusted with their care; He judges the false shepherds directly, 'Behold, I am against the shepherds' (v10), and He judges even within the flock, between the fat, pushy sheep and the lean, trampled ones (vv17-22). Yet His anger at bad shepherding flows from tender, personal love: He searches, gathers, binds up, strengthens, and finally shepherds them Himself.

crossReferences.note: Jesus claims the title and role this chapter promises directly, laying down His life for the sheep and uniting them into one flock under one shepherd.
Jesus' parable of the shepherd leaving the ninety-nine to seek the one lost sheep dramatizes exactly what God promises to do Himself in Ezekiel 34:11-12, 16.
Peter calls believers sheep who have returned to the Shepherd and Overseer of their souls, applying this chapter's language directly to Christ.
The Lamb Himself becomes the flock's Shepherd, guiding them to springs of living water, the ultimate fulfillment of God's promise to feed His flock in good pasture.

study.oneStory: This is the summit of the turn toward restoration: failed human shepherds are judged, and God Himself steps in to seek, gather, and tend His scattered flock, appointing His servant David as the one shepherd under an everlasting covenant of peace. Every page after this runs toward the Good Shepherd, Jesus Christ, who lays down His life for the sheep and gathers one flock under Himself forever.

sermon.title: The Shepherd-King
sermon.why: Keller preaches directly from Ezekiel 34:11-17 and 23-29, tracing the LORD's promise to shepherd His scattered flock Himself and to appoint His servant David as the one shepherd, exactly the movement from failed human shepherds to Christ the Good Shepherd this study is built around.

quiz.q: What is the specific charge against Israel's shepherds in verses 2-4?

options.text: They fed themselves, not the flock, neglecting the weak, sick, injured, strayed, and lost, and ruled with force and cruelty.
options.note: Verses 3-4 list this exact set of failures in detail.

options.text: They failed to build enough sheepfolds for the winter.
options.note: No mention of sheepfolds appears; the charge concerns neglect and self-serving cruelty, not infrastructure.

options.text: They demanded excessive tax payments from the flock.
options.note: The imagery is pastoral, not fiscal; the charge is about neglect and abuse of care, not taxation.

options.text: They abandoned the flock entirely and left the country.
options.note: They did not leave; they stayed and fed off the flock while failing to care for it, verses 3-4.

quiz.q: What does God Himself promise to do because of the shepherds' failure, verses 11-12 and 15-16?

options.text: Search for His scattered flock Himself, rescue them, and shepherd them, seeking the lost and binding up the injured.
options.note: Verses 11-12, 15-16 state this promise directly and repeatedly.

options.text: Appoint a committee of new human shepherds to replace the old ones immediately.
options.note: God promises to act Himself first, verse 11, before the later appointment of one shepherd in verse 23.

options.text: Destroy the whole flock and begin again with a new one.
options.note: The opposite is promised: rescue, gathering, and tender care for the existing flock.

options.text: Leave the flock scattered as a permanent judgment on their leaders' sin.
options.note: God explicitly reverses the scattering rather than leaving it permanent, verses 12-13.

quiz.q: What judgment does God pronounce between the sheep themselves in verses 17-22?

options.text: He will judge between the fat and lean sheep, holding accountable those who trampled pasture and muddied water needed by the weak.
options.note: Verses 18-20 describe exactly this trampling, muddying, and God's resulting judgment.

options.text: He will judge only the shepherds and never examine the sheep themselves.
options.note: Verses 17 and 20 explicitly extend judgment to the sheep, not only the shepherds.

options.text: He will reward the fattest, strongest sheep as the most faithful.
options.note: The opposite occurs: the strong who abuse the weak are judged and the weak are rescued, verses 16, 20-22.

options.text: No distinctions are made among the sheep at all.
options.note: Clear distinctions are made between fat and lean, rams and goats, verses 17, 20.

quiz.q: Who does God appoint as the one shepherd over His flock in verses 23-24?

options.text: His servant David, who will feed them and be their shepherd while the LORD is their God.
options.note: Verses 23-24 name 'My servant David' directly as the appointed shepherd and prince.

options.text: A newly chosen priest from the line of Levi.
options.note: The appointed figure is specifically called 'My servant David,' not a Levitical priest.

options.text: Ezekiel himself, appointed directly as Israel's shepherd.
options.note: Ezekiel is the messenger of this promise, not its subject; the appointed shepherd is 'My servant David.'

options.text: No shepherd is appointed; God rules the flock without any mediator.
options.note: A specific shepherd, David, is explicitly appointed, even while the LORD remains their God, verses 23-24.

quiz.q: What does the covenant of peace in verses 25-29 include?

options.text: Security from wild beasts, showers of blessing, fruitful land, and freedom from famine and the nations' scorn.
options.note: Verses 25-29 list each of these specific covenant blessings in turn.

options.text: Only a temporary ceasefire with surrounding nations.
options.note: The covenant is described as far more comprehensive than a political truce, covering safety, provision, and blessing.

options.text: The abolition of agriculture in favor of direct, miraculous provision.
options.note: The covenant promises fruitful trees and yielding land, verse 27, not the end of agriculture.

options.text: Nothing specific; the covenant's contents are left entirely undefined.
options.note: The covenant's contents are spelled out in specific detail across verses 25-29.

questions: Israel's shepherds fed themselves at the flock's expense. Where might you be using a position of care, leadership, or trust for your own benefit rather than for those God has placed under you?
God says, 'I Myself will search for My flock and seek them out.' How does knowing God personally, actively searches for the lost and wandering change how you view your own wandering seasons?
God judges between the fat sheep who trampled the pasture and the lean sheep left with only muddied water. Where might your comfort or plenty be coming at the expense of someone weaker near you?

Jesus claims this chapter's promise directly: 'I am the good shepherd; the good shepherd lays down his life for the sheep.' What would it look like, practically this week, to trust that He is actively shepherding you rather than leaving you to fend for yourself?
The covenant of peace promises security, provision, and freedom from fear. Where in your life do you most need to trust the Shepherd's covenant rather than your own vigilance?

translationNotes.note: 'My servant David' does not mean the historical King David returning to life; it is a prophetic title identifying the coming Messiah as David's promised heir, since the ministry and reign described here is everlasting.

'Covenant of peace' (berit shalom) names a single, comprehensive covenant of well-being, echoed again in Ezekiel 37:26 and Numbers 25:12, bundling security, provision, and right relationship with God into one promise.

Some translations render part of verse 31 as 'human sheep,' reflecting an unusual construction in the underlying Hebrew that simply emphasizes these are God's own people, His flock in the fullest covenantal sense, not merely animals under a shepherd.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched via the Makor scripture toolkit. Sermon link verified via live web search and fetch this session, Gospel in Life (Tim Keller), 'The Shepherd-King', preached directly from Ezekiel 34:11-17 and 23-29, matching this study's own key verses.
